spec_match.rationale: Encodes the exact predicate the spec change introduces — `flagged = bouncedQuickly && (accessedCookies || accessedStorage || writtenHttpCache)` — and contrasts it with the pre-v134 predicate. The 'EVADED' verdict path correctly only fires for cache-only + pre-133, which is the precise loophole closed.

interactive.rationale: Three independent checkboxes for the surfaces a tracker might use + a browser-version select drive a live verdict + log re-render. Algorithm is visibly different per row.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Card promises a toggle of tracker behaviour vs browser version with a flagged/evaded outcome; page delivers exactly that with a verdict + log.

edge_cases.rationale: The three EVADED / FLAGGED / NOT FLAGGED states are covered, but the simulator never demonstrates what happens when cache-only is selected on Chrome 134+ AND the user interacts during the bounce (should NOT be flagged) — the no-interaction predicate isn't surfaced at all.

references.rationale: see-also links chromestatus.com/feature/6299570819301376 and the PrivacyCG nav-tracking mitigations doc.

design_system.rationale: Verdict pills explicitly set both background and --bg-ivory foreground for each state (flagged: rose, cleared: emerald, evade: amber), so contrast is correct. All other styling via variables.

openQuestions.title: Add a 'user interacted with tracker' toggle
openQuestions.detail: The spec's flag predicate has two conjuncts: short stay AND no user interaction. The simulator hard-codes bouncedQuickly = true but never lets the user toggle the interaction flag, so the SSO/OAuth exception is invisible. Add a fourth checkbox 'tracker received user interaction during bounce' and AND it into the predicate so the carve-out is testable.

openQuestions.title: Document the grace period
openQuestions.detail: FLAGGED tracker state is purged 'after the grace period' — the verdict mentions this in text but never quantifies. Add a one-line note that the current grace period is approx 1 hour (verify against the spec or DevTools) so visitors understand the action is deferred.

openQuestions.title: Show what 'cache stash' actually looks like
openQuestions.detail: The flow shows 'tracker.example/r?u=...' but the URL never changes — and the whole point of the cache-only attack is that the URL encodes the user ID. Make the bounce URL include a uid query param that mutates per page-load so visitors see why cache keys leak identity.

openQuestions.title: Add a 'fingerprinting surface' future column
openQuestions.detail: The matrix table in tracker-survival-matrix has a 'Chrome 140 (hypothetical: + fp surface)' option. The same future-state could be modelled here as a fourth tracker surface (fingerprinting) so the demo doesn't suggest cache is the last loophole.

summary: Best of the four siblings — the predicate is shown in code, the verdict states are precise (FLAGGED / EVADED / NOT FLAGGED), and pre-133 vs 134 is a clean comparison. Missing: the user-interaction exception, without which the SSO carve-out is invisible.
